1.pericope_label: The Church Scattered; Philip in Samaria
1.synthesis: On the day of Stephen's death, a great persecution breaks out against the Jerusalem church; all except the apostles scatter throughout Judea and Samaria (Acts 8:1). Devout men bury Stephen; Saul ravages the church, house by house, dragging off men and women to prison (Acts 8:2–3). Those scattered preach the word wherever they go (Acts 8:4). Philip goes to a city in Samaria, proclaims the Christ, and the crowd gives attention to what he says—unclean spirits come out of many, the paralyzed and lame are healed, and there is great joy in the city (Acts 8:5–8).
1.historical_context: The geographical expansion in Acts 8 fulfills Acts 1:8's commission: 'Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' Samaritans were the historically despised half-Gentile population of central Palestine—Jewish-Samaritan enmity ran deep (cf. John 4:9; Luke 9:52–53). Philip is one of the Seven chosen in chapter 6—the same table-servant who becomes evangelist, showing that the distinction between service and word-ministry was functional, not permanent. The persecution that scatters the church becomes the mechanism of its expansion—Luke's irony is deliberate and theological.
1.literary_structure: The persecution narrative (vv.1–3) gives way to mission narrative (vv.4–8) through the pivot of v.4: 'those who were scattered went about preaching the word.' The structure embodies the Acts theme that no opposition can contain the gospel—in fact, opposition accelerates it. Philip's ministry in Samaria is the first Gentile-adjacent mission and serves as preparation for the Cornelius episode in chapter 10. The joy in Samaria (v.8) echoes the joy of the seventy-two in Luke 10:17 and points to the consistent Lukan linkage between gospel reception and communal joy.
1.theological_application: The scattering of the church by persecution and the consequent spread of the gospel is the paradigmatic pattern of Acts: what human opposition intends as suppression becomes divine propagation. The church in every generation of difficulty must hold this truth: suffering and displacement are not signs of God's absence but potentially the mechanism of his advance. Philip's willingness to take the gospel into Samaria—culturally hostile territory for a Jewish believer—models the Spirit-driven crossing of ethnic and social boundaries that the gospel requires. Every exorcism and healing in Samaria is a sign of the kingdom's arrival beyond the boundaries of Jewish expectation.
1.christology: Philip 'proclaimed to them the Christ' (v.5)—the content of the Samaritan mission is Jesus as the Messiah. The signs that accompany the proclamation (exorcisms, healings) are the continued works of the risen Christ through his Spirit-filled servant: the same works that characterized Jesus' Galilean ministry now extend to Samaria. The great joy in the city echoes the angelic announcement at Jesus' birth ('great joy for all the people,' Luke 2:10)—the joy of salvation has now crossed the ancient ethnic boundary, beginning the fulfillment of 'all the people.'

9.synthesis: Simon, a magician who has amazed the people and claimed to be someone great, believes and is baptized when Philip preaches (Acts 8:9–13). When the Jerusalem apostles send Peter and John to pray that the Samaritans receive the Holy Spirit (they had been baptized but the Spirit had not yet fallen), Simon sees that the Spirit is given through the laying on of hands and offers money for this authority (Acts 8:14–19). Peter rebukes him: 'May your silver perish with you, because you thought you could obtain the gift of God with money! You have neither part nor lot in this matter, for your heart is not right before God' (Acts 8:20–21). He calls Simon to repentance; Simon asks for Peter's prayers, fearing the consequences (Acts 8:22–24).
9.historical_context: Simon Magus became one of the most discussed figures in early Christianity; the second-century church fathers identified him as the founder of Gnosticism. His popular title 'the Great Power of God' (v.10) reflects a Hellenistic religious title claiming to channel divine energy. The delayed receipt of the Spirit in Samaria (they were baptized but the Spirit came only with apostolic laying on of hands) has generated debate about the relationship between water baptism and Spirit-reception; the most likely explanation is a Lukan theological emphasis on the continuity between Jerusalem and the new mission through apostolic mediation. 'Simony'—the buying and selling of ecclesiastical offices—takes its name from this episode.
9.literary_structure: The Simon narrative interrupts the Samaritan mission to introduce a negative test case: what does it look like when someone receives baptism without genuine faith and Spirit? Simon's subsequent request exposes that his 'belief' (v.13) was fascination with miraculous power, not surrender to the Christ proclaimed by Philip. Peter's sharp rebuke ('your heart is not right before God,' v.21) contrasts with the genuine reception in the city. The episode provides a warning against confusing access to the Spirit's gifts with the Spirit's transforming presence—a perennial distinction the church must maintain.
9.theological_application: The Simon episode addresses the persistent temptation to treat spiritual gifts and church authority as commodities that can be purchased or wielded for personal advancement. Peter's rebuke—'the gift of God' cannot be obtained with money (Acts 8:20)—establishes that the Spirit is not a resource to be controlled but a person to be received. Genuine faith is oriented toward the giver; Simon's orientation is toward the gift's utility. The church must examine whether its own uses of prayer, worship, and ministry are shaped by desire for the giver or desire for the gift's benefits. Simon's genuine fear at Peter's words and his request for prayer leave his repentance ambiguous—Luke does not resolve it.
9.christology: The gift of the Spirit that Simon covets is the risen Christ's gift to his church (Acts 2:33)—Jesus has received the Spirit from the Father and poured it out on those who believe. The apostolic laying on of hands is not magical transmission but prayerful reception of what the risen Lord gives. Peter's authority to rebuke Simon derives from his commission by the risen Christ and his own filling with the Spirit; his sharpness is the pastoral clarity of one who will not allow the community to mistake spiritual power for spiritual formation.

26.synthesis: An angel directs Philip to the road from Jerusalem to Gaza. There he meets an Ethiopian eunuch—a court official of the Candace, the queen of Ethiopia—reading Isaiah in his chariot on his way back from worshiping in Jerusalem (Acts 8:26–28). The Spirit directs Philip to join the chariot; Philip hears him reading Isaiah 53 and asks if he understands it. The eunuch invites Philip in and asks whom the passage is about (Acts 8:29–34). Philip begins with that scripture and tells him the good news about Jesus (Acts 8:35). Seeing water, the eunuch asks to be baptized; Philip baptizes him (Acts 8:36–38). The Spirit carries Philip away; the eunuch goes on his way rejoicing (Acts 8:39–40).
26.historical_context: Ethiopia (Kush) was a prestigious kingdom south of Egypt; its queen's treasurer was a person of significant wealth and status. As a eunuch he could not be a full proselyte to Judaism (Deuteronomy 23:1) but was likely a God-fearer who worshiped at Jerusalem. Isaiah's promise of welcome for eunuchs in Isaiah 56:3–5 makes the christological interpretation of Isaiah 53 particularly poignant: the one who was previously excluded is the first to receive the salvation of the Servant who bore exclusion for others. The passage he reads (Isaiah 53:7–8) is the LXX version, about the sheep led to slaughter who did not open his mouth.
26.literary_structure: The episode is the first individual conversion narrative in Acts (Philip's Samaria ministry was communal). Its structure—divine direction → scriptural encounter → Christological explanation → baptism → departure in joy—becomes a template for subsequent conversion accounts. The Isaiah 53 passage is the only OT text explicitly quoted and interpreted in a conversion scene in Acts, making it Luke's definitive statement about the evangelistic use of scripture. Philip's disappearance by the Spirit (v.39) underlines that the episode was entirely divinely orchestrated.
26.theological_application: The Ethiopian eunuch's conversion breaks three barriers simultaneously: ethnic (African Gentile), bodily (eunuch, previously excluded from the assembly), and social (foreigner from beyond Israel's horizon). The gospel's first convert from the ends of the earth is precisely the one who would have been furthest from inclusion in the old covenant order. Philip's Spirit-directed availability—'rise and go' (v.26), 'go over and join this chariot' (v.29)—models attentiveness to divine promptings in ordinary movements. The eunuch's question 'about whom does the prophet say this?' is the ideal evangelistic opening: scripture already at work in a searching heart, needing only the Christological key to open it.
26.christology: Philip 'beginning with this Scripture... told him the good news about Jesus' (Acts 8:35)—the suffering servant of Isaiah 53 is identified with the crucified and risen Jesus. This is the earliest explicit record of evangelistic Christological exegesis of the Servant Songs, and it confirms the interpretive key the risen Christ opened on the Emmaus road (Luke 24:27). The one who was 'led as a sheep to slaughter' and whose life was 'taken up from the earth' (Acts 8:32–33; Isaiah 53:7–8) is the risen Lord who now sends his Spirit to direct his servants to find the searching and bring them home.
